critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. Let me work through the potential issues systematically.

**Issue 1: Label quality as a function of detection capability creates circular evaluation**

The claim is that the four labeling signals (SOC review, user reports, sandbox, TI signatures) produce ground truth labels for the test set that are independent of the features the transformer exploits.

The mechanism of failure: Sandbox detonation and threat-intelligence signature matches are themselves downstream of prior detections — they fire on known-bad infrastructure, known payloads, or behavioral patterns that security vendors have already seen. Spear-phishing, by definition, is targeted and low-volume. The most sophisticated spear-phishing (novel campaigns, clean infrastructure, no payload) will systematically fail all four labeling signals except SOC analyst review, and SOC review is typically triggered by user reports or upstream detections. The result is that the 'ground truth' labels are biased toward detectable phishing — phishing that left a signature trace — while the hardest cases go unlabeled or are absorbed into the benign class. The model is then evaluated on cases it had an implicit advantage on structurally. The 7-day dwell time helps with enrichment lag but does not resolve the fundamental issue that undetected phishing is missing from the positive class.

What would constitute evidence: Compare the distribution of positive-label sources in the test set. If >80% of positives have TI signature matches or sandbox hits (as opposed to SOC-only or user-only labels), the ground truth is biased toward already-characterized attacks. A complementary analysis: take a random sample of emails labeled benign, have analysts perform deep review on a subset, and measure the false-negative rate in the 'benign' class.

**Issue 2: Temporal split asymmetry in the validation/test ratio creates an implicit validation-set bias**

The claim is that the 25%/25% validation/test split (months 13–15 and 16–18) provides equivalent held-out conditions for model selection and final evaluation.

The mechanism of failure: All hyperparameter tuning and threshold optimization is performed on months 13–15. In a non-stationary threat landscape, the model is being tuned to the specific distribution of months 13–15, and final evaluation is on months 16–18. This is actually intentional and correct for deployment simulation. However, the 50/25/25 split means the validation period is only 3 months — small enough that a single large phishing campaign in that window could dominate PR-AUC and drive hyperparameter choices toward configurations that happen to catch that campaign's signature. The test set is then evaluated on a different 3-month window. The design acknowledges non-stationarity as a reason for temporal splitting but doesn't address the consequence: validation-period campaign composition becomes a confounder for model selection. This is a genuine but bounded issue — it doesn't invalidate the design, but it means the validation PR-AUC is a noisier signal than implied.

What would constitute evidence: Report PR-AUC on validation and test sets broken down by campaign cluster. If validation-set performance is dominated by 1–2 large campaigns and test-set performance diverges significantly, this is evidence the hyperparameter selection is campaign-overfitted. Walk-forward validation across multiple 3-month windows within the training period would give a more stable hyperparameter selection signal.

**Issue 3: The 512-token truncation creates a silent failure mode for spear-phishing specifically**

The claim is that truncating email body text to 512 subword tokens is an appropriate preprocessing step that preserves signal relevant to phishing detection.

The mechanism of failure: Spear-phishing emails are specifically adversarially crafted. A common evasion technique is to prepend large amounts of legitimate-looking text (copied from real conversations, legal boilerplate, news content) to push the malicious payload — the urgency prompt, the credential-harvesting link, the wire-transfer instruction — past the 512-token window. BERT's tokenizer truncates from the tail, so the attack payload is systematically discarded. The model then sees only clean-looking preamble and correctly classifies the email as benign. This is not a hypothetical: adversarial padding is a documented technique for evading transformer-based classifiers. The ablation (transformer without body text) would not surface this failure because it doesn't have body text at all.

What would constitute evidence: Analyze the position distribution of URLs, imperative verbs ('click here', 'wire funds', 'reset your password'), and known phishing phrases within the token sequence for true positive and false negative cases. If false negatives have significantly later payload positions than true positives, truncation is causing systematic misses. A simple mitigation — using the last 512 tokens, or a sliding window with max-pool, or a hierarchical approach — would serve as an ablation.

**Issue 4: The baseline's features (sender reputation, headers) are available to the transformer indirectly via text content**

The claim is that sender domain reputation is 'masked from the transformer' and that 'features are kept orthogonal to isolate the contribution of text modeling.'

The mechanism of failure: Email body text frequently contains the sender's domain, reply-to addresses, embedded links with tracked domains, and organization names. A transformer operating on raw email text is not operating on reputation-masked input — it can learn to associate textual mentions of specific domains, URLs, or infrastructure with phishing labels. If high-reputation domains are rarely mentioned in phishing emails and low-reputation domains frequently appear as link targets in the body text, the transformer is implicitly re-learning a version of domain reputation from text. This means the 'text contribution' measured by the comparison is not purely linguistic; it may be a combination of linguistic modeling plus implicit reputation signal extracted from text. The ablation (transformer without body text, using only subject) partially controls for this, but subject lines alone don't fully isolate it.

What would constitute evidence: Apply URL normalization and domain masking to the email body before feeding to the transformer — replace all URLs with a generic [URL] token and all domain strings with [DOMAIN]. Re-run the comparison. If transformer performance drops substantially with this masking, a significant portion of the 'text advantage' was implicit domain reputation recovery, not linguistic pattern learning.

**Assessment of scope claims:**

The scope section is admirably honest. It limits claims to a single organization and time period, explicitly disclaims generalization, and flags that causal mechanisms would require further analysis. This is the strongest part of the design.

**Summary:**

Issues 3 and 4 are the most methodologically significant. Issue 3 (truncation) creates a systematic blind spot specifically for adversarially-crafted spear-phishing, which is the exact threat this system targets — making it a prerequisite-assumption failure (the model must see the malicious payload to classify it). Issue 4 (text-domain conflation) means the measured 'advantage of text modeling' could be partially an artifact of implicit reputation signal, undermining the ablation interpretation. Issue 1 (label bias) is real but partially mitigated by the multi-source labeling and 7-day dwell time. Issue 2 is bounded and acknowledged by the design's own non-stationarity argument.

None of these individually invalidate the experiment, but Issues 3 and 4 together mean the specific hypothesis — that transformer text modeling contributes generalizable linguistic signal beyond reputation and headers — is not cleanly testable with this design as stated.
all_issues_raised: Label quality is biased toward detectable phishing (TI signatures, sandbox hits), systematically excluding undetected spear-phishing from the positive class and inflating apparent recall.
512-token truncation from the head creates a systematic blind spot for adversarially padded spear-phishing where the malicious payload appears late in the email body.

The transformer can implicitly recover domain reputation signal from URL and domain strings embedded in email body text, meaning the measured text-modeling advantage conflates linguistic signal with implicit reputation re-learning, undermining the ablation.
Validation-set hyperparameter selection on a 3-month window may be dominated by single large campaigns, making model selection noisy relative to the test-set distribution.
all_issues_adjudicated: Label quality is biased toward detectable phishing (TI signatures, sandbox hits), systematically excluding undetected spear-phishing from the positive class and inflating apparent recall.
512-token truncation from the head creates a systematic blind spot for adversarially padded spear-phishing where the malicious payload appears late in the email body.

The transformer can implicitly recover domain reputation signal from URL and domain strings embedded in email body text, meaning the measured text-modeling advantage conflates linguistic signal with implicit reputation re-learning, undermining the ablation.
Validation-set hyperparameter selection on a 3-month window may be dominated by single large campaigns, making model selection noisy relative to the test-set distribution.
